meta.title: Born to Trade
meta.legacyPath: 睡着过项目组/2. 所有文章(11.20)[192篇]/142. P2 - Born to Trade 交易的本能/
meta.legacyFilename: 142. P2 - Born to Trade 交易的本能.html
meta.originalTitle: Born to Trade 交易的本能

children.text: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 14-26, which are based on Reading Passage 2 on the following pages.
Long before our ancestors had any trappings of civilisation, they had a taste for material goods. Kate Douglas discovers where this ancient obsession has led us.

Humans are born to trade and we don't need shops or money to do it—the heart of commerce is an instinctive ability for what anthropologists call “reciprocity”. This is an ability to exchange goods of equal worth, and it evolved as the brains of our ancient ancestors and societies became more complex, allowing individuals to keep a running tally of their interactions with others. Evidence from modern hunter-gatherers leaves little doubt that the exchange of food and favours is innate, as is the ability to keep track of the credits and debits that accrue as a result. Combine this skill for mental book-keeping with even the most basic material culture, and trade inevitably follows.

Once trade gets off the ground, the economic benefits quickly make it irresistible. But exchanging tools, food and other essentials of life in a barter economy is a far cry from the shopping mall. Modern consumerism extends beyond subsistence and utilitarianism to encompass everything from Gucci handbags and BMW convertibles to valuable paintings. Clearly, the worth of such goods is not inherent but resides in certain intangible qualities that we invest in them. When did humans start holding these goods in such high esteem?

All the archaeological evidence for the flowering of consumer culture has up to now pointed to a date of around 40,000 years ago. That's when early modern humans started making increasingly intricate bone and stone tools, carving patterns into rocks and creating representational art such as carved figurines, jewellery and cave paintings. However, discoveries in Africa are pushing the origins of consumerism much further back into human prehistory.

A few years ago, reports began to emerge of discoveries made at the Blombos Cave, a site of ancient human habitation in South Africa. Christopher Henshilwood and colleagues from the University of Bergen in Norway dated thousands of pieces of ochre from the cave, and many of them proved to be more than 100,000 years old — before the time that early humans moved out of Africa. Ochre, a coloured clay that comes in various shades from red to black, does not occur naturally around Blombos and must have been imported from quarries at least 30 kilometres away, either directly by Blombos residents or through trade. Although ochre can be used to dry and preserve (cure) animal hides, the researchers are convinced the Blombos ochre had a symbolic purpose. For a start, it is predominantly red—any of the other colours available would have done for curing—and the surfaces of the clay had been scraped in a way that indicates they were used to yield pigment for dyes.

An even more intriguing discovery from Blombos was of 41 beads dating from 76,000 years ago, found in clusters and made from the shells of a tiny mollusc. These cannot be natural deposits, argue the researchers, as each cluster contains shells of a similar size and colour with consistently placed holes. What's more, all the beads display a pattern of wear suggesting friction from rubbing against thread, clothes or other beads.

The previous oldest find of beads in Africa dates back to just 45,000 years ago. And it seems the Blombos people's taste for beautiful items was not an isolated phenomenon. Jessica Thompson of Arizona State University in Tempe has described finding shell fragments from a site in Tanzania that she believes may represent debris from bead manufacture. They are at least 45,000—and possibly 280,000—years old. Although there is no evidence of how the ancient beads were used, their modern counterparts are often traded. So it looks as though our taste for jewellery and art is much older than we thought.

But why did we come to value these objects in the first place? In many animal species, individuals signal their genetic fitness by showing off with attention-grabbing adornment. Some researchers think that in humans, consumer products play a similar role. Archaeologist Aimee Plourde, from the University of California, also argues that even in egalitarian societies, some people are more successful than others. Among our ancestors, superior skills in areas such as hunting, crafts, environmental knowledge and contact with neighbouring groups would have brought respect—in other words, prestige.

And because prestige brings social benefits, people would want to show off their talents. The best way to do this would be through demonstrating possession of material items that are hard to fake. ‘A good hunter, for instance, could advertise his skills by wearing the tooth of an animal that is elusive or dangerous,' says Plourde. The benefits of prestige would also lead to competition to acquire it. As a result, the value and variety of prestige goods would spiral and there would be a parallel increase in the ranking of social systems.

If Plourde is correct, prestige goods form a direct link between our innate drive for trade and the development of structured, hierarchical societies. They are arguably the first step on the road to modern civilisation, paving the way for agriculture and urbanisation. We may not be impressed by beads any more, but their modern equivalents have the same fascination. Nobody believes the guy who spends €670,000 on a Bugatti Veyron car does so because he needs to travel at 250 mph. We all know in today's consumer society he's buying an exclusive status symbol.
Choose the correct heading for each section from the list of headings below.
Write the correct number, i–vii, in boxes 14–18 on your answer sheet. (Drag a heading from this list and drop it onto the correct section's answer box on the left.)

children.label: i The desire to make expensive jewellery
ii The clues that indicate ancient items were decorative
iii Querying the changes in the value of traded items
v How status can be gained from ownership of goods
vi Recent finds alter theories about when consumerism started

explanationSection.text:
题目 14
答案：iv
PDF 定位：iv A natural human talent 为每个段落选择合适的标题。A段的标题是"人 与生俱来的才能力量"。
PDF 解析：*the heart of commerce is an instinctive A段整段都在说"互惠（reciprocity）是一种本 ability for what anthropologists call 能/天赋"，能记账、能对等交换→"自然的人 'reciprocity'.. keep a running tall... keep 类才能"。 track of the credits and debits* (第 A 段)

题目 15
答案：iii
PDF 定位：ili Querying the changes in the value of B段的标题是"质疑/探问交换物品价值的 变化"。 traded items
PDF 解析：*Modern consumerism extends beyond 先对比"以物易物"与现代消费，再直接发问"人 类何时开始如此看重这些东西?"-正是对"价 subsistence.. Clearly, the worth of such goods is not inherent... When did humans 值变化"的追问。 start holding these goods in such high esteem?"（第B段)

答案：vi

题目 17
答案：ii
PDF 定位：li The clues that indicate ancient items D段标题"指向古物为装饰品的线索"。 were decorative
PDF 解析：一连串"证据/线索"：成嵌、同尺寸同颜色、 *beads.. found in clusters... shells of a 打孔位置一致、磨损痕迹→表明是穿缓假数 similar size and colour with consistently 的"装饰"。 placed holes... pattern of wear suggesting friction from rubbing against thread, clothes or other beads." (第 D 段)

children.text: Choose the correct letter, A, B, C or D.
Write the correct letter in boxes 19–21 on your answer sheet.
In section A, ‘reciprocity' refers to our ancestors' ability to
keep written records of different goods traded
monitor the balance between goods given and received
produce more materials for trading purposes
make money from trading goods and services
What is said about the ochre found at Blombos Cave?
It is of a type only found in this particular area.
It has all been dyed to give the same shade of red.
It has been perfectly preserved despite its age.
It appears it was selected for its colour.
What made the researchers realise the purpose of the beads found at Blombos Cave?
They are of many different shapes and sizes.
They were found in large numbers, clustered together.
They have patterns in many different colours.
They have holes and markings suggesting they were joined together.

explanationSection.text:
题目 19
答案：B
PDF 定位：在A段中，"reciprocity"指祖先具备哪种能力？
PDF 解析："keep a running tall... keep track of the credits and debits that accrue as a result." (第 A 段) 借贷往来)，对应Bmonitorthebalance..。不是书 面记录(A)，也非生产更多商品（C)或赚钱(D)。

题目 20
答案：D
PDF 定位：关于在布隆博斯发现的赭石（ochre)，文中说了什么？
PDF 解析："it is predominantly red... surfaces... scraped... 研究者认为这些赭石主要呈红色且被刮削出颜料，说明 used to yield pigment for dyes." (第 C 段) 看重其颜色/颜料用途，对应D。A与文意相反（赭石并 非本地天然产，系运入)，B"都被染成同一红色"文中未 述，C完好保存"亦无据。

题目 21
答案：D
PDF 定位：研究者如何意识到珠子的用途？
PDF 解析："similar size and colour with consistently placed 关键证据是规则打孔+摩擦磨损痕迹，显示曾被串联/ 穿戴，对应D。B"成簇"只是产状，不能直接推用途； holes... pattern of wear suggesting friction from rubbing against thread, clothes or other beads." A、C与文证不符。 (第D段)

children.text: from the passage for each answer.
Some researchers argue that we may use ownership of desirable goods to demonstrate our strength and fitness as other
use their attractive physical features. Showing off one's superiority is not a new phenomenon and even in ancient times successful people would have gained
from this. A desire for status could have led to an increase in the worth of prestige goods and to
among people wishing to achieve this status.
It is thought that our natural desire to trade provided a basis for organised farming and

development and finally resulted in the complex societies which can be seen today. Whilst we do not value things such as beads nowadays, other items hold the same appeal for us and bring the same status as owning beads did for our

题目 22
答案：species
PDF 定位：，借助外在吸引力一样" "像其它，
PDF 解析："In many animal species, individuals signal 先说"许多动物物种"会用显眼装饰展示适应 their genetic fitness by showing off with 度，后转到人类以商品完成相同功能一-与题干 attention-grabbing adornment. Some "attractive physical features"完全对应。 researchers think that in humans, consumer products play a similar role.

答案：respect
PDF 定位："古代成功者会因此获得
PDF 解析：*..superior skills... would have brought "展示优越"带来别人对你的"尊重/声望"。词表 respectin other words, prestige." 给出respect，与原文同义词组"prestige"并 列印证。

题目 24
答案：competition
PDF 定位："并引发人们之间的，
PDF 解析："The benefits of prestige would also lead to 原文用competition；词表提供抽象名词 competition to acquire it." competitiveness。语义一致：追逐声望→竞 争上升。

题目 25
答案：urbanisation
PDF 定位："为有组织的农业与， 发展奠基"
PDF 解析：urbanisation=城市化/城市发展，填入city形 *..paving the way for agriculture and 成自然搭配"citydevelopment（城市发展）"。（比 urbanisation." settlementsdevelopment更地道，也与 "urbanisation"严格同义。）

答案：ancestors
PDF 定位："带来与古时珠子对我们， 相同的地位"
PDF 解析："We may not be impressed by beads any 段落先对比"我们如今不再看重珠子"，再提"祖 more, but their modern equivalents have 先"时代的地位由此而来；题干"asowningbeads the samefascination."（同段前文）"Among "指向祖先，与选项ancestors did for our 完全匹配。 our ancestors, superior skills... would have brought respect....
